objectives: In this lesson, the student will learn how to
explain the concept of globalisation and how it is
applied in the UAE and around the world.
lessons.content: Moral, Social & Cultural Studies Unit 1 Trade, travel and communications: the UAE in an increasing globalised and interconnected world; cultural exchange Understand the concept of ‘globalisation’, some of the issues it raises and how it affects the UAE. Look at this image. Express what it could represent. In this lesson, the student will learn how to explain the concept of globalisation and how it is applied in the UAE and around the world. • Understand the concept of ‘globalisation,’ some of the issues it raises and how it affects Instruct students first to examine the globe image, and then to answer the questions. The image does not contain any place names. This is to illustrate the possibility of connecting with all parts of the world. Students should complete the exercise by writing a sentence about globalisation. This task aims to familiarise students with the term ‘globalisation’ and to encourage them to think about globalisation and its impact. • I think globalisation is exciting because we now can communicate with one another, share culture through travel and trade and transport products around the world in a • I think globalisation is dangerous because we are part of a huge global economy where something that happens in one area of the world can have knock-on effects worldwide. • Following this activity, help students come to an agreement on how to define globalisation.
activities: Activity 1 (10 min): Instruct students first to examine the globe image, and then to answer the questions. The image does not contain any place names. This is to illustrate the possibility of connecting with all parts of the world. Students should complete the exercise by writing a sentence about globalisation. This task aims to familiarise students with the term ‘globalisation’ and to encourage them to think

Activity 2 (15 min): Instruct the students to read the text. Ask the students to sit in pairs and discuss what they think are the most important ideas in this text. Ask them to write down what are the three most important aspects of globalisation from their viewpoint. Suggested Answers a. Free trade, free movement of labour, more travel opportunities, wider range of connections, dilution of national identities

Activity 3 (10 min): Ask students to read the text. Discuss the points they have read and encourage them to talk about their experience in travel and tourism. Divide the class into 4 groups and ask each group to answer one of the questions. When they are done, share the answers with the class and allow discussions. Suggested Answers a. Tourism supported global peace by allowing travelers to learn about other cu

Activity 4 (10 min): A core activity requiring students to read the article and then complete the task. Think- pair - share: The text aims to introduce the different types of connections that exist between people. It also encourages students to think about connections with people outside the classroom. Each group’s answers can be written on the board, and then used to form the basis of a more detailed group dis

Activity 5 (10 min): Globalisation may be perceived by young people as essentially the result of the development of technology. This text helps them realize that for years sports have brought people together, through international events such as the Olympic games or the football World Cup. a. More recently, Special Olympics founded by Eunice Kennedy Shriver has contributed to this. Mrs. Shriver was inspired

Activity 6 (15 min): Suggested Answers Environment has become a global issue. Actions done in one country affect many others. Finding environmentally sound sources of energy has become a worldwide concern. All rights reserved. objectives: In this lesson, students will explore the factors
that enabled the growth of globalization in the
modern world: communications, transport, trade
freedom, labor availability, the elimination of tariff
barriers and establishment of regional blocs such
as the European Union, the Cooperation Council
for the Arab Gulf states, economic blocs and
international organizations.
lessons.content: Moral, Social & Cultural Studies Understand the concept of ‘globalisation,’ some of the issues it raises and how it affects the UAE. Understand the concept of a sustainable global economy, and the challenges it faces. Understand some economic concepts. Unit 1 Trade, Travel and Communications: The UAE in an increasing Globalised and Interconnected World; Cutural Exchange Your teacher is going to read out the statements below. When the teacher reads one out, stand up if you agree with it. Stay seated if you disagree. 1. Workers have benefited from globalisation. 2. The dominance of English as a worldwide language is a result of globalisation. 3. People around the world now have similar tastes in food and clothing. 4. Globalisation affects the environment. Take a Stand on Globalisation In this lesson, students will explore the factors that enabled the growth of globalization in the modern world: communications, transport, trade freedom, labor availability, the elimination of tariff barriers and establishment of regional blocs such as the European Union, the Cooperation Council for the Arab Gulf states, economic blocs and international organizations. • Understand the concept of ‘globalisation,’ some of the issues it raises and how it affects • Understand the concept of a sustainable global economy, and the challenges it faces. • Understand some economic concepts. This activity encourages students to think about how they communicate and use social media. Read out the statements one by one.
activities: Activity 1 (5 min): This activity encourages students to think about how they communicate and use social media. Read out the statements one by one.

Activity 2 (10 min): This activity is designed to lead the students to relate the concept of globalisation to one of the elements which without it could not be possible. Suggested Answers a. Communications: without fast communications countries, companies and even individuals would not be able to organise efficiently international trade or do business in general; communications have shortened distances and sav

Activity 3 (10 min): After students read the text, make sure they have understood the definition of multinationals. Ask them to name multinationals they know of: this could be famous sport shoe, soft drink manufacturers, fast food chains, etc. Divide the class in groups and ask each group to choose a multinational. Allow them to search the internet to find out about the country of origin of the multinational ea

Activity 4 (10 min): Instruct the students to read the text about the formation of regional trading blocks. Ask several students one after the other to cite the name of some of these blocs. Suggested Answers a. Expansion of markets, increased competition, economies of scale, increased foreign investment. b. Many economists believe that regional trade blocs favour their own region instead of the whole world.

Activity 5 (5 min): Instruct the students to read the information on about sustainability. With reference to the diagram below, explain that the three conponents are inter-related and inter- dependent. Explain that the word sustainable is often used in the phrase “sustainable development” which means development of a country, town or village that can keep going over time, enabling the place to become self reli

Activity 6 (5 min): It is advisable to have this activity done in groups. Social: The social pillar means essentially the people. To have sustainability it is crucial to ensure the well-being of the people who are the engines of all economic and environmental activities: Education- health- elderly people. Environment: Endangered species- solar energy. Economy: Portal for job search; space program; research and

Understand the concept of a sustainable global economy, and the challenges it faces. Understand some economic concepts. Evaluate the impact of communication technology on the individual and society. People (or consumers) have needs and wants, so businesses meet those needs and wants and charge money for doing so. In the same way that the students asked themselves questions during the peer market, companies ask themselves the same questions. The answers to these questions inform the number of items made (produced) and their price. Businesses supply products to meet demand. Scarcity drives up the price of goods.
activities: Activity 1 (10 min): Ask students to find positive or negative effects of globalisation ona given country. Suggested Answers This activity is intended to demonstrate that an issue that arises as a result of globalisation could be deemed positive or negative. It depends on who it affects. Positives Negatives Jobs More opportunities for foreigners Less opportunities for locals Travel Easier, faster, more place

Activity 2 (15 min): This activity introduces students to the concepts of trade, supply and demand and basic price mechanism. These will help them understand some of the concepts addressed in the coming activities. After students have completed the peer market activity, direct students to think about and analyse the experience: encourage students to consider how their experience with the peer market plays out in r

Activity 3 (10 min): Introduce the topic explaining that in the next two segments we will be exploring the positive and then the negative sides of globalisation. Globalisation has been a good thing for many developing countries who now have access to many markets and can export cheap goods. Globalisation has also been good for multi- national corporations. But globalisation has not always been good for working p

Activity 4 (10 min): Ask the students to read the text then ask them to choose one of the points they have just read. Group those who have chosen the same point and ask them to elaborate on it in front of the class. For Beginners Elaborate on the point itself by giving concrete examples For Advanced Students Counter-argue and tie them with advantages in the previous activity. Activity 5 (10 min): This activity can be given in pairs or groups, depending on the strength of the class group. The questions range from lower order questions (what do you see?) to higher-order critical- thinking questions (why do you think they are feeling this way?). Key terms: Astana, nomadic, Soviet Union, energy exports, diversification.
